item.title: Fiji High Commission urges nationals in India to stay vigilant
item.description: The Fiji High Commission in New Delhi has issued a public advisory urging Fijian nationals in India to exercise increased caution amid ongoing protests and student demonstrations in several parts of the country. In the advisory, the High Commission said protests could lead to traffic disruptions, road closures and occasional clashes with law enforcement.
item.content:encoded:
The Fiji High Commission in New Delhi has issued a public advisory urging Fijian nationals in India to exercise increased caution amid ongoing protests and student demonstrations in several parts of the country.

In the advisory, the High Commission said protests could lead to traffic disruptions, road closures and occasional clashes with law enforcement.

Fijians are being advised to avoid protest sites and large public gatherings, follow instructions from local authorities, and allow extra travel time due to possible traffic diversions.

The High Commission is also encouraging citizens to monitor local news and official updates before travelling.

It said the mission is continuing to closely monitor developments and reminded Fijian nationals requiring urgent consular assistance to contact the Fiji High Commission in New Delhi.

The advisory concludes by urging all Fijians in India to remain vigilant and prioritise their safety.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Fiji gets solar-powered vaccine carriers
item.description: The Ministry of Health and Medical Services has launched a pilot programme that will deploy 65 solar-powered vaccine carriers across Fiji, aimed at improving vaccine delivery to remote communities and strengthening the country’s emergency preparedness. Assistant Minister for Health Penioni Ravunawa launched the Solar Vaccine Carrier Pilot Programme, describing it as a major step towards improving
item.content:encoded:
The Ministry of Health and Medical Services has launched a pilot programme that will deploy 65 solar-powered vaccine carriers across Fiji, aimed at improving vaccine delivery to remote communities and strengthening the country’s emergency preparedness.

Assistant Minister for Health Penioni Ravunawa launched the Solar Vaccine Carrier Pilot Programme, describing it as a major step towards improving the “last mile” of Fiji’s immunisation services.

The solar-powered carriers will be distributed to 65 health facilities and are designed to maintain the required vaccine cold chain temperature of between 2°C and 8°C for up to 12 hours.

“This pilot will assess the feasibility, acceptability, and operational effectiveness of the technology as we continue to strengthen the ‘last mile’ of immunization services and ensure every child has equitable access to life-saving vaccines.”

Designed specifically for Fiji’s conditions, the lightweight carriers can be worn as backpacks and include a dedicated compartment for a solar panel and charger. They can be powered by either electricity or solar energy, reducing reliance on conditioned ice packs during vaccine transport.

The Assistant Minister said the technology would also improve Fiji’s ability to maintain immunisation services during natural disasters.

“These solar-powered carriers will help maintain vaccine potency in the field, reducing disruption to immunization services and improving our resilience during emergencies.”

He noted that during cyclones, floods and other emergencies, vaccines often have to be relocated to prevent cold chain failures and wastage.

Mr Ravunawa commended the Ministry’s Expanded Programme on Immunization team, frontline healthcare workers and development partners for embracing the innovation.

“This is an important step towards a smarter, more resilient, and people-centred immunization system, with more sites planned in the future.”

He added that every child deserves access to safe and effective vaccines, regardless of where they live or the challenges faced in delivering healthcare services.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Security key to tourism growth, says Police Commissioner
item.description: Police Commissioner Rusiate Tudravu says maintaining a safe and secure environment is critical to sustaining Fiji’s tourism industry and attracting investment, as a new police post was officially opened at Port Denarau Marina. The new Port Denarau Marina Police Post was commissioned yesterday, through a partnership between the Fiji Police Force, Port Denarau Marina Pte Limited and Flame Tree Ltd.
item.content:encoded:
Police Commissioner Rusiate Tudravu says maintaining a safe and secure environment is critical to sustaining Fiji’s tourism industry and attracting investment, as a new police post was officially opened at Port Denarau Marina.

The new Port Denarau Marina Police Post was commissioned yesterday, through a partnership between the Fiji Police Force, Port Denarau Marina Pte Limited and Flame Tree Ltd. The facility was made possible after Flame Tree Ltd purchased and refurbished a container, while Port Denarau Marina provided the land.

Speaking at the opening, Commissioner Tudravu said the collaboration reflected the growing commitment of the private sector to supporting law enforcement.

He said the police-private partnership supports his strategic vision of “Stability and Growth”, noting that security underpins confidence in Fiji’s economy.

“The private sectors’ support towards law enforcement is sure to create stability leading to growth. Stability in security leads to investor confidence, growth in tourism sector, healthy visitor arrivals and more employment opportunities.”

The Commissioner said Denarau remains one of Fiji’s busiest tourism hubs, serving as a gateway to many of the country’s island destinations, making a strong police presence essential.

He also pointed to Fiji’s tourism performance, saying visitor numbers demonstrated continued confidence in the country’s safety.

“Contrary to things being said about the safety and security of our nation, Fiji’s visitor arrivals say otherwise as the month of June 2026, holidaymakers dominated visitor arrivals, accounting for 81 per cent of all visitors.”

“Security is an important criterion in choosing a holiday destination, and it takes collaboration such as this to create that conducive environment for visitors to come and enjoy our Fijian hospitality.”

Port Denarau Marina General Manager Cynthia Rasch echoed the importance of security to the tourism industry, saying it supports visitor confidence and business growth.

The event also marked the opening of a new jetty at the marina, increasing berth capacity for vessels. Mrs Rasch said the expansion would create greater demand across the tourism sector and generate more employment opportunities.

The Fiji Police Force thanked the business community for its continued support in strengthening law enforcement capabilities and enhancing public safety.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Fijian convicted rapist arrested by U.S. immigration authorities
item.description: A Fijian national convicted of rape has been arrested by United States Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) as part of a nationwide operation targeting convicted child sex traffickers, sexual predators and drug traffickers. In a statement issued on July 17, ICE identified the man as Nalesh Prasad, describing him as an “illegal alien” from Fiji who was convicted of rape with force or violence
item.content:encoded:
A Fijian national convicted of rape has been arrested by United States Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) as part of a nationwide operation targeting convicted child sex traffickers, sexual predators and drug traffickers.

In a statement issued on July 17, ICE identified the man as Nalesh Prasad, describing him as an “illegal alien” from Fiji who was convicted of rape with force or violence in Sacramento, California.

His arrest was among several announced by ICE involving individuals convicted of serious offences, including human trafficking of a minor for commercial sex, attempted forcible sodomy, drug trafficking and people smuggling.

Assistant Secretary Lauren Bis said ICE would continue targeting what she described as the “worst of the worst” despite a sharp rise in assaults against immigration officers.

“Just yesterday, ICE arrested child sex traffickers, violent sexual predators, drug traffickers, and other public safety threats who have no business remaining in our country.”

She added that anti-ICE rhetoric had contributed to a significant increase in assaults against officers but said the agency would continue its enforcement efforts.

“Despite these smears, ICE will continue to get criminals off our streets.”

According to ICE, the arrests form part of ongoing nationwide operations to identify and remove non-citizens convicted of serious criminal offences from communities across the United States.

ICE has also launched a public webpage highlighting arrests made under its “Worst of the Worst” initiative.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Human Rights Commission calls for probe into school religious incident
item.description: The Fiji Human Rights and Anti-Discrimination Commission has called for an immediate investigation into allegations that a student at a prominent secondary school was singled out because of his religion during a discussion led by an external visitor. In a statement, the Commission said reports and video footage circulating on social media raised serious concerns about child protection and religiou
item.content:encoded:
The Fiji Human Rights and Anti-Discrimination Commission has called for an immediate investigation into allegations that a student at a prominent secondary school was singled out because of his religion during a discussion led by an external visitor.

In a statement, the Commission said reports and video footage circulating on social media raised serious concerns about child protection and religious freedom, although it noted that the facts of the incident were still being established.

Director Loukinikini Lewaravu said any situation where a child feels targeted, pressured or humiliated because of their religion or beliefs must be treated seriously.

“Any situation in which a child feels targeted, pressured, humiliated or excluded because of their religion, beliefs, identity or background, raises significant human rights and child protection concerns.”

The Commission said every child has the right to learn in a safe, respectful and inclusive environment, free from discrimination, intimidation or coercion.

It stressed that child safeguarding extends beyond protection from physical harm and includes protecting children from emotional and psychological harm, bullying, discrimination and pressure relating to religion, culture or identity.

The Commission also reminded schools that Section 22 of the 2013 Constitution guarantees freedom of religion, conscience and belief, and provides that students cannot be required to receive religious instruction or participate in religious observances outside their faith without the consent of a parent or legal guardian.

It further cited Section 41 of the Constitution, which requires that the best interests of the child be the primary consideration in all matters affecting children.

The Commission is calling on the Ministry of Education to investigate the matter promptly and fairly and review policies governing external visitors and speakers in schools.

It has also recommended stronger child safeguarding policies, clear codes of conduct for external visitors, regular training on child rights and religious diversity, and accessible avenues for students and parents to raise concerns.

“Fiji’s rich cultural and religious diversity remains one of our nation’s greatest strengths. Our schools play a vital role in shaping future generations and fostering mutual respect, understanding and social cohesion.”

The Commission said it stands ready to work with the Ministry of Education and other stakeholders to strengthen child safeguarding measures and ensure every child can learn in an environment that protects their rights, dignity and wellbeing.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Judge says child responsibility comes with legal responsibility
item.description: Lautoka High Court judge has stressed that “child responsibility and legal responsibility go hand in hand” while sentencing a grandmother whose negligence led to the death of her three-year-old grandson in Sigatoka. Delivering sentence on July 20, Justice Sunil Sharma sentenced the grand-mother to two years imprisonment, suspended for three years, after she pleaded guilty to one count of manslaugh
item.content:encoded:
Lautoka High Court judge has stressed that “child responsibility and legal responsibility go hand in hand” while sentencing a grandmother whose negligence led to the death of her three-year-old grandson in Sigatoka.

Delivering sentence on July 20, Justice Sunil Sharma sentenced the grand-mother to two years imprisonment, suspended for three years, after she pleaded guilty to one count of manslaughter arising from a breach of duty.

The court heard that on January 29 this year, she crossed a pedestrian crossing with one grandchild, leaving three-year-eight-month-old son about 10 metres behind. The child entered the crossing unattended and was struck by a vehicle, later dying from his injuries.

Justice Sharma said the loss of the child could have been avoided.

“Child responsibility and legal responsibility go hand in hand, and any breaches would have serious consequences.”

He added that while the accused would carry the pain of losing her grandchild forever, the court could not ignore that an innocent life had been lost.

“No amount of repentance will bring back the victim.”

Justice Sharma said the accused had shown genuine remorse, pleaded guilty at the earliest opportunity, cooperated fully with police and accepted responsibility for her actions.

He also noted she continued to care for her other grandchildren without incident, demonstrating that she had learned from her mistake.

The court ruled that these factors made immediate imprisonment inappropriate and justified a wholly suspended sentence.

In addition to the suspended prison term, the mother was ordered to complete two sessions of general and parental counselling through the Ministry of Women, Children and Social Protection within three months.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Strong El Niño to persist as Fiji waters cool: Met Office
item.description: A strong El Niño is expected to persist for the remainder of 2026, with the Fiji Meteorological Service forecasting cooler-than-normal sea surface temperatures across much of Fiji’s waters between August and October. In its latest Fiji Ocean Outlook , the Fiji Met Service says below-normal sea surface temperatures are likely around the Western and Central divisions, Kadavu and the southern Lau Gro
item.content:encoded:
A strong El Niño is expected to persist for the remainder of 2026, with the Fiji Meteorological Service forecasting cooler-than-normal sea surface temperatures across much of Fiji’s waters between August and October.

In its latest Fiji Ocean Outlook , the Fiji Met Service says below-normal sea surface temperatures are likely around the Western and Central divisions, Kadavu and the southern Lau Group, despite above-normal temperatures being recorded across Fiji’s waters during June.

The report says the South Pacific Convergence Zone (SPCZ) is also expected to shift south of its normal position, just outside Fiji’s Exclusive Economic Zone, a movement that can influence the distribution of skipjack tuna across the Pacific.

Despite the ongoing El Niño, the outlook brings positive news for Fiji’s reefs, with the four, eight and 12-week coral bleaching outlook remaining at “No Stress” across Fiji and Rotuma.

The Met Service also forecasts below-normal sea levels across most of the Fiji Group during the August to October period, while Rotuma is expected to experience near-normal sea levels.

The report notes that El Niño conditions are typically associated with reduced rainfall in Fiji, often increasing the likelihood of prolonged dry conditions across the country.

Read Today’s Fiji Times Read Todays Fiji Times Today's Paper Subscribe Trending Stories Investigation nears end Grower urges military deployment to save crushing season $650m debt write-off defended Former inmates urged to return to villages The walk for hope © 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Judge orders Nigerian national access to phone evidence
item.description: The High Court has ruled that a Nigerian man accused of importing nearly five kilograms of methamphetamine must be allowed to inspect the mobile phone at the centre of the prosecution’s case, saying the move is necessary to protect his right to a fair trial. In a ruling delivered on July 20, Justice Aruna Aluthge granted an application by Chukwuemeka Phabian, who sought access to the phone belongi
item.content:encoded:
The High Court has ruled that a Nigerian man accused of importing nearly five kilograms of methamphetamine must be allowed to inspect the mobile phone at the centre of the prosecution’s case, saying the move is necessary to protect his right to a fair trial.

In a ruling delivered on July 20, Justice Aruna Aluthge granted an application by Chukwuemeka Phabian, who sought access to the phone belonging to prosecution witness Viliame Nalagi, an alleged accomplice who was later granted immunity.

The prosecution intends to rely on communications extracted from Nalagi’s phone to allege that Phabian is the person identified as “Pastor Thomas” in conversations linked to the alleged importation of 4.8685 kilograms of methamphetamine. Phabian denies both the charge and that he is “Pastor Thomas.”

Justice Aluthge said the existing disclosure framework in Fiji does not adequately address electronic evidence and highlighted the importance of ensuring digital evidence can be independently verified.

He ruled that digital evidence is vulnerable to alteration and that the defence is entitled to inspect the device to test its authenticity and integrity.

“Adopting an explicit disclosure and forensic inspection framework for digital devices is crucial to prevent miscarriages of justice.”

The judge also found that the prosecution’s disclosure of extracted data on a USB did not remove its obligation to ensure the defence could properly examine the evidence.

He said denying access would prejudice the accused’s fair trial rights more than it would affect the privacy interests of the immunity witness.

However, Justice Aluthge limited the inspection to communications directly relevant to the criminal charge, saying the defence must not be allowed to conduct a “fishing expedition” through the witness’s private data.

The inspection is to be carried out at a controlled location under the supervision of the Fiji Police Digital Forensics Unit.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.
